title: Gum arabic and ink consistency
subtitle: Adjusting calligraphy ink viscosity for different nibs and paper surfaces
excerpt: The consistency of calligraphy ink determines how the nib loads, how the stroke forms, and how quickly the ink dries on the paper. Gum arabic thickens ink that has become too watery; distilled water thins ink that has dried too stiff. Understanding how to adjust both substances before touching the paper saves time correcting letters after the fact.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on inks and media for calligraphy. Johnston discusses gum arabic as a binder and thickener for calligraphy media. Internet Archive scan available.

glossaryTerms.definition: The resistance of a liquid to flow. High-viscosity ink is thick and moves slowly; low-viscosity ink is thin and flows quickly.
The resistance felt in the nib as it moves across the paper when the ink is too thick or the paper surface is too rough.

content.text: Calligraphy ink is not one fixed substance. The same bottle of sumi or iron-gall ink will behave differently in dry weather compared to humid weather, on smooth paper compared to rough, and on a broad-edge nib compared to a pointed nib. Learning to adjust ink
viscosity
with gum arabic and distilled water is one of the most practical skills in dip-pen work.
What you need
Working ink supply: sumi or iron-gall ink in a shallow inkwell, not in the original bottle. Always decant working ink into a separate well so the main supply is not contaminated.
Gum arabic: liquid gum arabic in a small dropper bottle. One or two drops per 5 ml of ink is sufficient to change its working properties noticeably.
Eyedropper: for adding distilled water or gum arabic to the working well in precise small amounts.
Test paper: a spare sheet of cartridge at the same weight as the working paper.
The move
Dip the nib into the working ink. Make a test stroke on the spare paper. Observe: does the stroke feel dragged (
ink drag
)? Does the stroke flood? Does it break up mid-stroke?
If the ink drags or is too thick to flow on hairline strokes: add one drop of distilled water to the working well. Stir gently. Retest.

If the ink floods or bleeds past the stroke edge: add one drop of liquid gum arabic to the working well. Stir gently. The gum arabic increases viscosity and also helps ink adhere to sized or smooth paper.
Retest after each addition. A single drop changes the working consistency noticeably.
What gum arabic does in practice

Gum arabic raises viscosity, slows drying time slightly, and helps ink stick to slick surfaces. A small addition is useful when writing on sized or hot-press watercolour paper, which resists standard sumi ink. It is also useful for calligraphy gouache. A drop of gum arabic stirred into thinned gouache stabilises it and stops the binder separating from the pigment in the nib.
Common mistakes

attrs.symptom: Ink dries in the nib between strokes
attrs.cause: The working ink is too thick or the room is too dry; ink in the nib evaporates quickly.

content.text: Add a single drop of distilled water and re-test. If the ink keeps drying in the nib during a long session, dip more frequently rather than thinning further, which risks flooding.

attrs.symptom: Too much gum arabic added; ink is stiff and does not flow in the nib
attrs.cause: Gum arabic was added in excess, turning the ink into a paste.

content.text: Thin the over-gummed ink with several drops of distilled water. If recovery is not possible, discard the working supply and decant fresh ink.
